Lab Guide › Writing & Dissemination

Present

How to design and deliver an effective, audience-appropriate talk.

  • Start guide: Undergraduates
  • Start guide: Graduate students
  • Start guide: Postdocs

  • Attending a conference
  • Applying for funding
  • Request recommendation letters
  • File your dissertation

All talks should be curated and adjusted to your current audience — undergraduates, graduate students, hiring managers, and so on. Ask who your audience is before you design the talk. (With thanks to Jean-Luc Dumont and Doug Altshuler, whose emphasis on clear communication inspires this page.)

Consider your audience

What is their level of expertise? How formal is the talk? What technical depth are they comfortable with? What questions will they have? Is the goal to educate or to persuade?

Determine your key takeaways

Every audience has two questions: so what? and who cares? Answer them as succinctly as possible. Your audience will remember only a few salient points — the Rule of Three is a good default, but don’t invent a third point to round things out. Simpler is always better.

Avoid presenting your research like a detective novel. Your audience should know immediately why they should care, or you lose their attention.

Graphics, data, and color

  • Make all graphics and data legible to every audience member.
  • Use color schemes consistently and intentionally, and ensure they’re accessible to a colorblind audience (accessible palette generator).
  • Always use vector graphics (.svg or .pdf); high-quality .png if you must.
  • Keep graphics directly relevant; build up complex diagrams with transitions; avoid laser pointers.
  • Be deliberate with data: avoid tables where possible, label all axes and keys, and build up data-heavy graphs in parts. Watch the lie factor and data-to-ink ratio.

Craft your slides

A typical talk includes a title slide, introduction/background, a roadmap/outline, methods, results, conclusions, future directions, and acknowledgements.

Do: use a legible font at a large size; keep backgrounds simple (plain white preferred); keep a consistent format; use color or weight to highlight takeaways; use vector graphics. Avoid: irrelevant images, too many bullet points, full sentences in bullets, and distracting color schemes.

  • Title slide: title plus all authors and affiliations; a simple eye-catching image or video and any logos. Treat it as a placeholder — don’t talk to it.
  • Roadmap: always include one and refer back to it throughout; use creative graphics, not a list-based outline.
  • Body slides: simple and to the point; include page numbers; build up bullets as you speak; use “appear” or “fade” transitions.
  • Transition slides: large fonts and contrasting colors to distinguish sections.
  • Conclusion: acknowledge contributors and funding (check with Christina), highlight key takeaways, and outline future directions. End on a confident note so the audience knows when to applaud.
